title: Wet-felted coasters set
subtitle: Four flat round coasters around 10 cm in diameter, single colour or with a contrasting border ring
excerpt: Wet-felt a set of four flat round coasters from merino roving. The method relies on even layer thickness and consistent rubbing to keep the discs flat during fulling. Each coaster takes about 30 minutes to full; the whole set can be made in a single afternoon sitting.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the standard wet-felting flat-panel tradition. Coaster-scale flat pieces are a common introductory project in guild workshops; the method is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for felting. The staples lie roughly parallel along the ribbon's length; thin wisps pulled from the end lay flat and felt evenly.
The vigorous agitation phase of wet felting that follows initial bonding. Rolling, rubbing, and throwing compact the fibres fully and shrink the piece to its final size.

Wool that has bonded lightly enough to hold its shape but is still somewhat spongy. At pre-felt the surface can be handled without falling apart; more working time is needed to reach the firm density of a finished coaster.

content.text: Four flat wet-felted coasters at around 10 cm in diameter make a practical and attractive table set. The main challenge in a flat coaster is keeping the disc flat and even during

thickness causes the thicker areas to shrink more slowly, which makes the disc curl or warp. Laying the roving in thin cross-hatched layers and rolling consistently in all directions prevents this. Each coaster starts at roughly 13 cm across and shrinks to approximately 10 cm; the 25 per cent linear shrinkage is consistent with merino at normal felting temperature. A contrasting border ring can be added by placing a thin ring of roving in a second colour at the outer edge of the layout before wetting.

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour for border (optional)
Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)

content.text: Make all four coasters in sequence. The instructions below describe one coaster; repeat for each. Working one at a time allows each coaster to be fulled to the correct size and flatness before moving to the next.

Lay a cotton tea towel flat on the worktop. Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay them in a rough circle about 13 cm in diameter on the towel. The first layer runs horizontally across the circle; the second layer runs vertically, crossing the first at 90 degrees. Each layer should be thin enough to be translucent when held to a light. Two cross-hatched layers at 10 g total give a coaster of good weight and density. If you are adding a contrasting border, lay a thin ring of the second-colour roving around the outer 1 to 2 cm of the circle on top of the two main layers.
Step 2: Wet down and begin rubbing

Mix olive-oil soap into hot water to make a light suds. Pour or spray the soapy water over the layout from the centre outward to saturate it without disturbing the arrangement. If using a felting net, lay it over the surface and press through the net. Rub through the net with flat hands in circular motions for 4 to 5 minutes, keeping the surface hot. Remove the net when the surface begins to hold together.

After the initial rubbing, lift the wet layout carefully off the towel and check the underside. Thin patches will show as near-transparent areas; thick patches will show as lumps. If you see uneven areas, pull thin wisps of dry roving and press them onto the thin spots, then wet and work those areas specifically before continuing. Getting the thickness right at this stage prevents the disc from warping later.

stage, transfer the disc to the bamboo rolling mat. Roll the mat up around the coaster and roll it back and forth on the worktop with firm, even pressure for 3 to 4 minutes. Unroll, rotate the coaster 90 degrees, re-roll, and roll again. Rotating the piece prevents the shrinkage from running in only one direction, which would produce an oval rather than a circle. Every few minutes unroll the mat, flip the coaster over, and check both the flatness and the diameter. If one edge is curling up, lay that edge down on the mat and press down firmly before re-rolling. Continue until the coaster reaches approximately 10 cm in diameter and feels firm and flat throughout, typically 20 to 30 minutes.

Rinse the coaster in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge it into cold water for 30 seconds. Press flat between both palms. Lay the coaster on a dry flat surface, pressing the edges down firmly, and leave to dry. If the disc continues to curl at the edges while drying, place a heavy flat object (a board, a book) on top. Once dry, the coaster should be fully flat and stay so in use. Trim the edge with sharp scissors to a clean circle if any stray wisps extend beyond the perimeter.

items.problem: The coaster is oval rather than round
items.cause: The rolling direction was not rotated regularly, so the piece shrank more along one axis
items.fix: Rotate the piece 90 degrees every 3 to 4 minutes of rolling. Checking the shape frequently and adjusting the rolling direction as soon as one dimension is noticeably shorter prevents the oval from setting in.

items.problem: The coaster is dishing or cupping after drying
items.cause: One face was worked more than the other during rubbing, or the piece was dried upright
items.fix: Work both faces equally, flipping the coaster regularly during rubbing. Dry flat on a hard surface with a weight on top if necessary.

items.problem: The contrasting border colour has migrated into the main colour
items.cause: The border roving was worked too aggressively before the fibres had begun to lock
items.fix: During the initial rubbing phase, work the centre of the coaster more than the border. The border fibres will bond naturally once the centre is at pre-felt stage. Merino migrates readily; a narrow 1 cm border will always blend slightly at the inner edge.

items.problem: The disc shrank to less than 10 cm across
items.cause: Over-fulling, or the starting diameter was less than 13 cm
items.fix: Measure the layout before wetting; it should be at least 13 cm in diameter. Check the size during fulling every few minutes and stop when it reaches the desired diameter.

content.text: Felt coasters are hand-wash only. Rinse under cool running water if they become marked, press flat, and dry flat. Machine washing will shrink them further and may warp the shape. The felt surface is naturally water-resistant and will repel a small amount of condensation from a cold glass; a very wet or hot cup may transfer moisture through over time. If a coaster gets waterlogged in use, press it flat between dry towels and dry flat away from direct heat. Do not iron felt; the fibres compress unevenly under direct dry heat.
